title: Born Into a Church Home in Flint
topic: Birth and Paternal Roots
summary: Interviewed in Seattle on April 17 by David Cline for the Library of Congress, the Smithsonian, and the Southern Oral History Program, Reverend Dr. Samuel Berry McKinney opens by placing his birth on December 28, 1926, in Flint, Michigan, and his father, a Baptist minister born near Gainesville, Georgia, in 1892, one of twelve children of a Georgia sharecropper named Wade Hampton McKinney.

title: From the Fifth Grade to Morehouse
topic: Father's Late-Started Education
summary: McKinney recounts his father, at eighteen and only in the fifth grade, being encouraged by his Gainesville pastor toward education, entering Atlanta Baptist College in 1910 (renamed Morehouse College in 1914), earning a high school diploma in 1916 and a Morehouse B.A. in 1920, then going on to Rochester Theological Seminary.

title: Two Sisters in a Church Basement
topic: Founding of Morehouse and Spelman
summary: He traces the schools' origins: an 1867 Augusta Baptist college that became Atlanta Baptist and then Morehouse, named for Henry Lyman Morehouse of the Northern Baptist Convention, and a women's college that Packard and Giles started in the basement of Friendship Baptist Church, becoming Spelman after their schoolmate, married to Laura Spelman Rockefeller, helped fund it.

title: A Spelman Girl From Birmingham
topic: Mother and the Atlanta University System
summary: McKinney describes the 1929 reorganization that created the Atlanta University system, with Morehouse for men and Spelman for women keeping their autonomy, and introduces his mother, Annie Ruth Berry, the youngest of six who came from Birmingham to Spelman in 1916 and was among the few women who technically graduated from Morehouse before Spelman itself became a college.

title: Let Down Your Bucket Where You Are
topic: Booker T. Washington and Owning Land
summary: He explains how many Alabama Black families, including his maternal grandfather, took Booker T. Washington's counsel to let down your bucket and own land, and notes that when Dr. King marched from Selma to Montgomery, every stop sat on land owned by Black people, so King was never an intruder.

title: Raised by Black Puritans
topic: Mother's Family and Marriage
summary: McKinney tells how his mother, orphaned of her own mother at age two, was reared by a seventeen-year-old sister among women he calls Black puritans who stood for moral purity against the notion that any Black woman was prey for white men, and traces his mother's teaching at Jackson College and Roger Williams College up to her 1924 marriage and his father's first church in Flint.

title: A Desire for Justice, Planted at Morehouse
topic: Antioch and the Social Justice Ethic
summary: He notes his father's 1928 call to Antioch Baptist Church, which he served thirty-four and a half years until his death in January 1963, and describes how Morehouse graduates, able to teach only at Black colleges, carried south an emphasis on social justice, shaped under President John Hope, so that the later emergence of Martin Luther King Jr. was no surprise.

title: Religion Functions on the Front End
topic: The Social Gospel and Rauschenbusch
summary: McKinney defines the social gospel through the passage from Isaiah that Jesus read in Luke about being anointed to free the captives, names Walter Rauschenbusch, who taught at Rochester and died while his father was still a student, and notes Benjamin Elijah Mays of South Carolina becoming Morehouse president four years before he himself entered.

title: A Fifteen-Year-Old Squirt Named King
topic: Entering Morehouse With M.L. King
summary: He recalls entering Morehouse in 1944 at seventeen, facing the draft at eighteen, and a freshman class of over four hundred against only fifty upperclassmen, in which the youngest was a little fellow from Atlanta nicknamed Squirt, the fifteen-year-old Martin Luther King Jr., who had skipped twelfth grade under a wartime Georgia program, and remembers King as a city student who drove a car and never won an oratorical contest.

title: Watching His Father Speak Out
topic: A Cleveland Boyhood
summary: McKinney describes growing up in Cleveland from eighteen months old, watching his father confront injustice rather than walk away from it, and the notable guests his father hosted, including A. Philip Randolph, Walter White of the NAACP, and Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, president of Howard and a Morehouse mentor to his father.

title: Don't Patronize Where You Cannot Work
topic: Boycotts, War Industry, and the GI Bill
summary: He recalls Cleveland's Future Outlook League boycotting milkmen and the transit system to force hiring, the wartime industrial boom and second great migration his parents were part of, his father's fifty-nine days in the Army in World War I, and the GI Bill that he calls the best the country ever had and that put both him and his brother through college.

title: German Prisoners at the Tablecloth
topic: Segregation in the Army Air Corps
summary: Drafted into the Army Air Corps and trained at Sheppard Field, Texas, McKinney recounts a lunch stop in West Texas where Black soldiers in U.S. uniform ate in a colored dining room while German prisoners of war sat at tablecloths with silverware in the white dining room, a sight that left him asking how far it was to Cleveland and thinking about going AWOL.

title: Squadron F and a Dollar for a Salute
topic: Daily Life on a Segregated Base
summary: He describes the enlisted rule against saluting anyone under captain, officers who paid a dollar to the first enlisted man who saluted them, being assigned to Squadron F where all Black servicemen were grouped regardless of trade, segregated colored and white USOs, and hitchhiking troubles getting back to base past hostile Southern whites.

title: From Mississippi for a Night
topic: Discharge and a Pre-Law Track
summary: McKinney tells of faking a Mississippi origin to get a hitchhiking ride from a Southern white driver, the Army halting overseas shipment of Black soldiers, reenlistment pay-line indignities, and the rule that 1945 draftees be out by Christmas 1946, after which he resumed a pre-law track at Morehouse and went on to Colgate Rochester Divinity School.

title: An Ambulance in the Valley or a Fence
topic: Kelsey, Mays, and a Calling
summary: He recounts how a required Bible course under George Kelsey turned him from law to ministry, with Kelsey's teaching that the law functions on the back end while religion functions on the front end and his image of whether to put an ambulance in the valley for those who fall or a fence at the cliff's edge, and remembers Benjamin Elijah Mays, president of Morehouse from 1941 to 1967, who told students to note the name and badge number of any white streetcar driver who wronged them and report it, modeling the witness for justice he taught.

title: Finding the Longest Extension Cord
topic: Joe Louis as a Community Hero
summary: He evokes how the community rallied around Joe Louis, with neighbors running extension cords to bring radios onto front porches so no one missed a fight, the same way they followed Jackie Robinson and Cleveland's Larry Doby, and recalls a four-hundred-pound visitor from Mississippi shouting down his aunt's porch when Louis knocked out Max Schmeling, a victory the Jewish community shared since Schmeling was one of Hitler's.

title: On Your Mark in Front of Jesse Owens's House
topic: Role Models and a Path to Ministry
summary: McKinney recalls Jesse Owens's family living a few doors north in Cleveland, where children would line up at a starting crouch passing his house, the Cedar Avenue YMCA that reached nearly every Black youth, and the models who shaped his sense of ministry, his father, Mays, and Mordecai Johnson, in a father's church that at its height held over three thousand members and hosted Adam Clayton Powell Jr.

title: His Last Trip Anywhere Alone
topic: Inviting King to Seattle
summary: He tells of inviting Martin Luther King Jr. to speak on the brotherhood of the church in 1961, a visit King told him was the last trip he would ever take by himself, certain that assassins were out there after the near misses and escapes he had already had.

title: We Don't Want Your Money
topic: First Presbyterian Reneges
summary: With the 1962 Seattle World's Fair making his own facilities too small, McKinney recounts arranging to use the large downtown First Presbyterian Church, then receiving a letter barring the event for supposedly skipping protocol, and a meeting where a white-haired lawyer heading the church council coldly forbade it and offered to reimburse his advertising, which he refused, vowing to tell the world the truth and walking straight down the hill from Eighth and Madison to another building where the event was successfully held.

title: An Apology Thirty-Eight Years Late
topic: First Presbyterian's Letter of Apology
summary: McKinney recalls almost tossing a typed envelope just before his 1998 retirement that turned out to be First Presbyterian apologizing for the injustice done decades earlier, the younger minister who came in person, and his own reply that white people owe an apology not for owning slaves but for the white privilege by which certain things come automatically.

title: Better Late Than Never
topic: Reparations and Japanese Internment
summary: He argues that reparations, modeled on the apologies and payments made to Japanese American families interned after Pearl Harbor, whose seized farmland he could see from the hills, are part of a prophetic healing that names injustice and makes forgiveness possible, and responds to James Forman's 1969 Black Manifesto call for reparations as part of people's eyes opening to four hundred years of slavery.

part: The Color Line in the Northwest
title: Illegal to Settle in Oregon
topic: The Pacific Northwest's Racist Roots
summary: McKinney insists no part of the country escaped the poison of racism, citing an Oregon territory law making it illegal for Black people to permanently settle, not repealed until 1926, and sketching the Louisiana Purchase that Jefferson bought from Napoleon after the Haitian revolt, the carving of some twenty states from it, and a stretch of Highway 99 north of Seattle once called Confederate Highway.

part: The Color Line in the Northwest
title: Fasten Your Seat Belt
topic: George Washington Bush and a Sober Heckler
summary: He tells of George Washington Bush leading a wagon train west from Missouri, barred from settling in Oregon and crossing to Fort Vancouver toward Puget Sound, and of a drunken heckler at a Washington event who demanded to know what Blacks had contributed to the state, whom McKinney promised to leave sober by the time he finished, noting the slow bumpkin enforcement that escorted Black settlers county to county.

part: The Color Line in the Northwest
title: Saved From Becoming British Columbia
topic: Bush, the Pig War, and the Border
summary: McKinney recounts Bush's company crossing the Columbia to settle near present-day Olympia amid complaints the neighborhood was changing, the boundary dispute settled at the 49th parallel that stranded Point Roberts so its schoolchildren bus sixty miles a day, the Pig War that nearly brought conflict with Britain, and his retort to the heckler that without this Black man crossing the border, Washington might have become part of British Columbia.

title: Where Your Members Migrated From
topic: Arriving at Mount Zion and Migration Patterns
summary: Called in late November 1957 and beginning the first Sunday of February 1958, McKinney offers younger ministers his method of learning where a congregation migrated from, tracing how Providence drew Virginians, Cleveland drew Georgians and Alabamians, Chicago drew Mississippians, and Seattle drew people from west of the Mississippi, Louisiana, Texas, Arkansas, and Oklahoma.

title: The Juneteenth Factor
topic: Emancipation, Juneteenth, and a Post-Racial Myth
summary: He explains the Juneteenth factor he first met in Army basic training in Texas, contrasting the January 1, 1863, Emancipation Proclamation and the watch-meeting services that counted down to it with the news not reaching Texas until June 19, 1865, and links the lag, and modern talk of Texas secession over Obama, to a Civil War he frames as less about freeing the enslaved than breaking the union.

title: A Vein of Coal Under the Pews
topic: How Black People Came to the Area
summary: McKinney notes that before World War I only two Army forts, Fort Lewis and one in New Jersey, let interracial couples live there, dates his church's 1890 founding to a year after Washington statehood, and traces the region's coal economy, the Welsh and Scottish miners buried young in a mass grave after an explosion, the Black scab laborers brought to Roslyn to break the union and to Kennydale, and the transcontinental railroads that drew people west.

title: They Could Tell From My Voice
topic: Housing Discrimination in Seattle
summary: He acknowledges that beneath Seattle's better surface lay real prejudice, recalling restaurants that had to be reminded he might refuse their tainted food and the difficulty finding housing, with landlords asking if he was colored over the phone, until his wife Carol, a purist in speech more English than the British, would call and a different welcome would await when they arrived.

title: The March on Frederick & Nelson
topic: Opening Jobs and the Founding of CACRC
summary: McKinney describes marching on the downtown department store Frederick and Nelson, now the Nordstrom building, to open it to Black employment, names the Urban League and NAACP as allies with CORE arriving later, and recounts helping organize in 1963 the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, a coalition of groups in which Reverend John Adams, later an AME bishop, was the most successful leader.

title: Too Close in the South, Too High in the North
topic: Two Fronts and a Slow State Board
summary: He recounts a school strike and stay-at-home over segregated, inferior schools under the Central Area Civil Rights Committee, working on two fronts in harmony with the South while fighting local racism, captures the difference in the saying that the South did not care how close Blacks got so long as they did not get too high while the North did not care how high so long as they did not get too close, and notes a slow state anti-discrimination board and a once-critical congregant who was first to apply when the Frederick and Nelson march succeeded.

title: Too Radical or Not Radical Enough
topic: Friction With the Black Panthers
summary: McKinney reflects that some always think you too radical and others not radical enough, and describes the late-1960s rise of more militant groups including a Black Panther chapter, the strained relations between them and the ministers some Panthers saw as targets of their anger, his sense of being played one group against another, and his belief that the Panthers could have been more effective than they were.

title: Park the Cars and Babysit the Children
topic: Buying Land and a Church Credit Union
summary: Asked about Liberty Bank, the Opportunities Industrialization Center, and the Samuel Berry McKinney Manor, he begins with his drive to buy land so Mount Zion could build with parking and child care to grow rather than plateau, contrasts it with gentrification surrounding a sister church, and tells how, refused by local banks, the congregation established a credit union and built its sanctuary with help from the American Baptist Home Mission Society.

title: Reagan Pulled the Rug Out
topic: OIC, Liberty Bank, and Institution Building
summary: McKinney recounts building the Seattle Opportunities Industrialization Center on Twenty-Second and Jackson, founded in Leon Sullivan's movement to train and educate, whose funding President Reagan cut until Representative Jim McDermott had the state take it over on the verge of bankruptcy, and his part in organizing Liberty Bank at Twenty-Fourth and Union, framing his career as protesting and building alike.

title: Community When You No Longer Have Neighborhood
topic: Dispersal, Gentrification, and New Arrivals
summary: He frames a central problem of how to have community once a neighborhood disperses, looking to how Jewish and Asian communities stay united while scattered, recalls asking his congregation how many lived within a mile and finding the crowd thinned the closer in he asked, traces the two old Black pockets off Madison and Jackson that grew into the Central Area, and notes that the largest number of Black people now moving in come from across the African continent.

title: Africa Is Not a Country
topic: A Tense Meeting on Race and Tone
summary: McKinney recounts a man at a meeting expecting him to be crushed by the news that African Americans were now the third-largest non-white group, and his retort that what unites Hispanic people is not ethnicity but tone, so there are Black Hispanics, that the comparison lumped all Blacks against single segments of others, and that Africa is a continent with as many differences among its peoples as among whites.

title: An Offer I Couldn't Refuse
topic: Selling Property and a Wife's Home
summary: He tells of warning Central Area residents not to sell their valuable property, only to be asked by one who left whether he had not given that advice, answering that he too got an offer he could not refuse, and recounts moving in 1958 to Madrona for forty-two years before his wife found this house, which she marked with new floors and windows, the wife who passed in August.

title: They Did Not Count on the Coming Together
topic: Progress, Obama, and Division
summary: Reflecting on progress across his lifetime, McKinney rejects the idea that Obama's election settles everything, contrasting the reaction against Obama with the tolerance shown a Bush he calls the worst president, faulting Obama for bending too far toward people who would rather see the country suffer than credit him, and warning of the old trick of playing Blacks and Latinos against each other that did not reckon with communities coming together.

title: Praying That Sense Will Prevail
topic: Closing Hopes and the Archive
summary: He closes with cautious hope, supposing the country headed toward a woman president and that Hillary Clinton would do well, praying that the nuts and fools who feel their lives depend on guns will come to their senses and that uncommon sense will prevail, as the interviewers thank him and note that the material will go to the Library of Congress.
